verdict_reasoning: HSPD1 (Hsp60) is canonically a mitochondrial matrix chaperonin essential for protein folding. However, the NCBI summary explicitly notes function as 'a signaling molecule in the innate immune system,' and the literature documents cell-state-induced surface display: HSPD1 translocates to the outer leaflet during stress (heat shock, hypoxia, oxidative stress), cell death (apoptosis, necroptosis), and in tumor cells (cancer ecto-chaperone biology). Surface-biotinylation and flow-cytometry studies confirm outer-leaflet display under these conditions. Additionally, HSPD1 is an established danger-associated molecular pattern (DAMP) recognized by innate immune receptors (TLRs, LOX-1) when displayed on stressed/dying cells. Baseline compartment is mitochondrial matrix, but documented non-baseline PM translocation under stress and cell-death conditions qualifies as contextual via cell_state_induced mechanism.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Extent and kinetics of HSPD1 surface display in non-tumor, non-dying-cell contexts (e.g., infection, activation) not fully characterized; primary evidence base centers on stress, cell death, and cancer contexts. Unclear whether surface pool is stable enough to support binder engagement vs. transient immunogenic exposure.
